content:
Context: I'm trying to apply tag-std to unsafe code in Rust for Linux. Tag-std is a safety property checker, currently as a study for Rust's stdlib. Also see some links and backgrounds in #3.

I'm writting a rustc and rustdoc wrapper to include libsafety_macro.so when compiling rfl code.

But sometimes I got rust_minimal compiled in the first place, before any linux/rust/ code is compiled:
https://github.com/os-checker/tag-std/issues/5
It leads to compilation failure.

While sometimes I got the right order to compile, and everything just compiles.

So my question is why the order is out of control sometimes, and how to fix this.

Here's the make command:

BUILD_TARGETS="
    samples/rust/rust_minimal.o
"

make -C linux LLVM=1 -j$(($(nproc) + 1)) \
  RUSTC=$(which safety-tool-rfl) \
  RUSTDOC=$(which safety-tool-rfl-rustdoc) \
  $BUILD_TARGETS

full check script is here.
I've seen that happen if you compile a sample that is disabled in the config. Make sure that the sample is enabled.

I copied the config from rfl ci script in Rust lang repo, and CONFIG_SAMPLE_RUST_MINIMAL is set to m there.

Now I set the value to y, but rust_minimal is still compiled first.

Currently build config:

# Prepare Rust for Linux config
cat <<EOF >linux/kernel/configs/rfl-for-rust-ci.config
# CONFIG_WERROR is not set

CONFIG_RUST=y

CONFIG_SAMPLES=y
CONFIG_SAMPLES_RUST=y

CONFIG_SAMPLE_RUST_MINIMAL=y
CONFIG_SAMPLE_RUST_PRINT=y

CONFIG_RUST_PHYLIB_ABSTRACTIONS=y
CONFIG_AX88796B_PHY=y
CONFIG_AX88796B_RUST_PHY=y

CONFIG_KUNIT=y
CONFIG_RUST_KERNEL_DOCTESTS=y
EOF

# Merge linux config
make -C linux LLVM=1 -j$(($(nproc) + 1)) \
  rustavailable \
  defconfig \
  rfl-for-rust-ci.config

Targets such as rust_minimal.o in samples/rust/Makefileare not tied to target prerequisites in rust folder.
So it means the order is not guaranteed at all?

Maybe rust/kernel.o should be built first in one make command, and these samples are then built in another command.

Vague said:

Targets such as rust_minimal.o in samples/rust/Makefileare not tied to target prerequisites in rust folder.
So it means the order is not guaranteed at all?

There is a dependency. If you touch anything in the kernel crate and rebuild one of the samples, the kernel crate will be rebuilt.
Not sure where that dependency is added though.

I have this in the generated file .rust_minimal.o.cmd:

deps_samples/rust/rust_minimal.o := \
  /home/aeh/src/linux-rust/linux-next-build-x86_64-1.78.0/rust/libcore.rmeta \
  /home/aeh/src/linux-rust/linux-next-build-x86_64-1.78.0/rust/libcompiler_builtins.rmeta \
  /home/aeh/src/linux-rust/linux-next-build-x86_64-1.78.0/rust/libkernel.rmeta \
  /home/aeh/src/linux-rust/linux-next-build-x86_64-1.78.0/rust/libffi.rmeta \
  /home/aeh/src/linux-rust/linux-next-build-x86_64-1.78.0/rust/libpin_init.rmeta \
  /home/aeh/src/linux-rust/linux-next-build-x86_64-1.78.0/rust/libmacros.so \
  /home/aeh/src/linux-rust/linux-next-build-x86_64-1.78.0/rust/libpin_init_internal.so \
  /home/aeh/src/linux-rust/linux-next-build-x86_64-1.78.0/rust/libbuild_error.rmeta \
  /home/aeh/src/linux-rust/linux-next-build-x86_64-1.78.0/rust/libbindings.rmeta \
  /home/aeh/src/linux-rust/linux-next-build-x86_64-1.78.0/rust/libuapi.rmeta \

Yes, building a target does work, there is no need for a make first.

@Vague Could you please confirm it works for you when you don't have any wrapper or custom tooling?

I suspect your RUSTC etc. are not satisfying our rustavailable logic and thus Rust gets disabled automatically (this is confusing, but this is how we were requested to set it up originally -- we may change it eventually).

If that works, then check that you actually succeed on make ...... rustavailable adding your custom RUSTC (and please note that you should add it everywhere, not just in your last make).

That will also give you an explanation of why it may not be satisfied, which will probably help.

e.g.

$ make LLVM=1 RUSTC=test rustavailable
***
*** Running 'test' to check the Rust compiler version did not return
*** an expected output. See output and docs below for details:
***
If your wrapper behaves similar to a normal rustc, things should work. Otherwise, it may need tweaks either on your side or the kernel side.

Andreas Hindborg said:

I have this in the generated file .rust_minimal.o.cmd:

Thx for telling me that file. I just see it!

Miguel Ojeda said:

I suspect your RUSTC etc. are not satisfying our rustavailable logic and thus Rust gets disabled automatically

Yeah! That's the problem. Appreciate for the saver rustavailable.
I've tweaked the RUSTC wrappers by supporting --versionand variants. But it's my mistake in my script not to call rustavailable on wrappers, otherwise I should noticed a bug in wrappers.

Anyways, glad to know rustavailable and the output without right "RUSTC". :heart:
Vague has marked this topic as resolved.
